exemplar.title: In what ways do pedestrian activity, traffic patterns and land use differ with distance from the Central Business District in Stavanger, Norway?

detailedExemplarData.title: In what ways do pedestrian activity, traffic patterns and land use differ with distance from the Central Business District in Stavanger, Norway?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Precise, measurable fieldwork question clearly linked to theoretical models.
Geographic context pinpointed with multiâscale locational detail and feasible transect design.
Highâquality mapping following conventions with clear legends, scales, and inset maps.
weaknesses: Minor typographical error in theory name (âBitârentâ vs. âBidârentâ).
Zone labels on locator maps could be clearer to reduce visual clutter.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Every strand in Criterion A met the topâband descriptors: the question is geographical with strong theoretical links, the context is precisely defined for primary data, and maps adhere to conventions. Minor presentation suggestions do not detract from awarding the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive, stepâbyâstep method descriptions with clear theoretical alignment.
Robust primary data collection yielding reliable, highâquality data.
Effective controls (timing, observers) and systematic sampling ensuring consistency.
weaknesses: Justification for grouping certain vehicle sub-types could be expanded.
Could clarify rationale for grouping âother vehiclesâ within motorised counts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methodology is explained in depth and explicitly linked to the research question and underlying theory, and data collection is demonstrably reliable and extensive. This fulfills the topâband requirements for Criterion B, meriting the maximum 3 marks.

strengths: Extensive, relevant data sets covering three key variables across eight transects.
Use of varied, appropriate presentation techniques (scatterplots, stacked bars, buffers).
Statistical tables complement graphical data for enhanced clarity.
weaknesses: Occasional lapses in conventions: missing axis units, truncated titles, misaligned table headers.
Some symbols and statistical notation are unexplained in captions and legends.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are largely relevant and sufficient, and presentation employs effective graphical techniques. However, intermittent convention errors (captions, labels, formatting) prevent topâband (5â6) status and position the work solidly in the 3â4 band, justifying 4 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Advanced use of Spearmanâs rank, Chi-square, and regression analyses with stated confidence levels.
Insightful linking of statistical results to distanceâdecay, bid-rent, and Burgess models.
Comprehensive interpretation of patterns and anomalies, rejecting/accepting hypotheses with theory integration.
weaknesses: Standard errors or confidence intervals are not calculated, and minor gaps remain in addressing non-motorised data anomalies.
Degrees of freedom for Chi-square tests could be more explicitly detailed for full reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs written analysis demonstrates comprehensive statistical rigor (multiple inferential tests, confidence levels) and expertly ties findings back to geographical theory. While small methodological details are omitted, the overall depth and coherence of the analysis justify the topâband score of 8.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly synthesizes findings for all three hypotheses and directly addresses the research question.
Fully supported by earlier analysis and statistical results within word limit.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, concise, and entirely supported by the analysis, meeting all criteria for the maximum 2 marks.

strengths: Identification of relevant strengths (consistent timing, systematic sampling) and weaknesses (singleâday data, limited distance).
Relevant, actionable improvement suggestions outlined (weekend sampling, extended transects, added qualitative elements).
weaknesses: Discussion of how suggested improvements would quantitatively impact results could be more developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation appropriately outlines significant strengths and limitations with relevant suggestions for improvement, aligning with the middleâband (2 marks) descriptor. Although depth could be greater, the work surpasses superficial listing and merits 2 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Two wellâlabelled locator maps are included with north arrow, scale bars and inset; subsequent thematic maps (transects, pedestrian density, land-use rings) also follow cartographic conventions (legend, annotation).  
â¢  Although minor clutter exists, overall mapping quality is clear, earning the top-band judgment for spatial representation.

The student formulates a clear, focused geographical question that specifies three measurable variables and a defined spatial dimension (distance from Stavangerâs CBD).  
â¢  Explicit theoretical framing is provided through distance-decay, bid-rent and Burgess models, allowing testable hypotheses to be generated.  
â¢  The wording is precise enough to guide appropriate primary data collection, satisfying the descriptors for the top band.

A range of appropriate techniques (scatterplots with trend lines, stacked bars, choropleth-style buffers, proportional symbols) are used effectively to visualise each variable; statistical tables accompany graphs.  
â¢  While visually clear, some graphs lack axis units or titles are truncated, indicating occasional lapses, keeping the work within the 3-4 mark band.

Primary techniques yield extensive raw tables for pedestrians, motorised and non-motorised traffic and 95 land-use observations. Consistency measures (same weekday timeframe, systematic intervals) and adaptations for busy sites show care for reliability.  
â¢  The volume and execution of data collection satisfy the descriptor of âreliable, high-quality primary dataâ.

The analysis employs Spearmanâs rank, Chi-square and regression lines, with null/alternative hypotheses and critical value comparison.  
â¢  Confidence levels are identified (5 % and 0.1 %), but standard error or confidence intervals are not calculated, so the work meets âappropriate descriptive and statistical techniquesâ and some inferential depth, meriting 5 within the 5-6 band.

The student links trends and anomalies to models (distance decay, bid-rent) and offers plausible explanations (pedestrianised streets, supermarket effect).  
â¢  Most but not all anomalies are exhaustively discussed (non-motorised flat trend explanation limited), so the strand fits comfortably at the lower end of the 5-6 band.

The geographical context section locates Stavanger at city, regional and national scales, quotes current population data and describes economic background.  
â¢  The CBD is pinpointed (Domkirken cathedral and park) and eight 2-km transects are justified, demonstrating clear feasibility for primary data collection.  
â¢  This level of specificity fulfils the âprecise location with clear feasibilityâ requirement for the highest band.

The evaluation identifies several relevant strengths (consistent timing, systematic sampling) and weaknesses (single-day collection, limited distance) with brief explanation of their significance.  
â¢  The discussion is appropriate but not deeply developed, thus matching the middle band for depth.

Data sets are extensive and largely relevant to the question; three variables are captured across eight transects giving adequate spatial coverage for analysis.  
â¢  Minor gaps exist (non-motorised counts relatively small, 0-values in Chi-square), so the evidence aligns best with the 3-4 band rather than the top.

The conclusion clearly summarises findings for each hypothesis, links back to theory and answers the fieldwork question within 150 words.  
â¢  Assertions are directly supported by earlier statistical results, fulfilling the criteria for a fully supported conclusion (band 2).

All three hypotheses are tested, yet sections still contain minor gaps: non-motorised traffic dataset is acknowledged as inconclusive but not further interrogated; Chi-square 0 value issue is noted but uncorrected.  
â¢  Therefore analysis is âwith some gaps in evidenceâ, aligning with 3-4 band, and 4 is appropriate.

Methodology explains systematic point counts every 100 m, RICEPOTS classification, timing controls and justification linked back to the three theories.  
â¢  Each method is detailed step-by-step with rationale (e.g., two observers in high-traffic areas), demonstrating comprehensive explanation and theoretical alignment.

Figures are numbered and sources cited, but a few captions are partially cut, tables contain formatting errors and some symbols (Ï, Z) remain unexplained.  
â¢  These âoccasional errors in conventionsâ place the work in the 1-2 band for convention adherence.

Improvement suggestions (weekend data, longer transects, qualitative additions, adjust Chi-square classes) are relevant and outline expected impacts in short phrases.  
â¢  Although concise, they move beyond superficial listing, fitting the 2-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: The sustainable transport discussion in Figure 22 enriches the non-motorised analysis by contextualising infrastructure effects.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Consider quantifying âwell-plannedâ cycle routes with usage rates or lane-length per capita to move beyond descriptive commentary.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Conclusion succinctly synthesizes all three hypotheses and directly addresses the fieldwork question, reflecting comprehensive support.
The evaluation table lists valid improvements but lacks depth; detail how weekend versus weekday sampling quantitatively alters trends.
Strength: the consistency of data collection timing is clearly identified as enhancing validity across transects.
The fieldwork question is precise, framing three measurable variables and allowing testable hypotheses linked to geographical models.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 effectively locates Stavanger but could benefit from zone labels (e.g. 0â400 m, 500â900 m) and clearer colour differentiation to reduce visual clutter.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Typographical error: âBit-rent Theoryâ should read âBid-rent Theoryâ to align with the established urban land-value model.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Methodology description systematically links every procedure back to the research question and theoretical models, ensuring comprehensive relevance.
Traffic data categories are clear, but the distinction between motorised sub-types merits justification; consider detailing why âother vehiclesâ were grouped.
Reliance on Finn.no for rent data is valid, but online listings may omit unadvertised properties; triangulate with municipal or cadastral records.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The anomaly in pedestrian counts between 1000â1400 m and 1500â2000 m is noted but merits statistical treatment or exclusion rationale.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 12âs scatterplot effectively shows correlation but omits axis labels and units, reducing clarity for the reader.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Include a brief legend or notation explaining $$d$$ and $$n$$ in the Spearman formula to ensure all statistical terms are clear.
The interpretation of the Spearmanâs null-hypothesis rejection is well linked to exponential decay theory, strengthening your analysis.
To substantiate anomalies in pedestrianised streets, include photographic evidence or field sketches to enrich your qualitative interpretation.
Observed values in the Chi-square test are in percentages; converting back to raw frequencies improves accuracy of expected count calculations.
Table 4âs layout misaligns headers with columns; realign cells and ensure consistent decimal formatting in Chi-square calculations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The critical value comparison in Figure 15 is correctly used to reject Hâ; the linkage to bid-rent theory is insightful.
State how degrees of freedom (df=3) are calculated in your Chi-square test to clarify your methodology and ensure reproducibility.
Figure 16âs plot is clear; consider adding the regression equation and RÂ² value for land-use distance trends.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The discussion integrates Burgess and bid-rent models well, demonstrating a strong link between empirical findings and geographical theory.
Apply Spearmanâs rank to non-motorised data or justify omission statistically to substantiate the interpretation of its flat trend.
The works-cited list mixes citation styles; adopt a single convention (e.g., MLA or APA) for consistency and professionalism.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
